metadata.description: Chinese vocabulary for basic words, people, and place names

content.name: to_be
content.side_e: Wǒ shì xuésheng, wǒ bú shì lǎoshī.
content.side_f: I am a student; I am not a teacher.
content.side_g: 是 (shì) is the copula linking two nouns: A 是 B = 'A is B' (identity/equation), as in 我是学生 and 你是老师吗？Negate only with 不是 (never 很是/没是): 我不是老师。Do not use 是 before adjectives — say 北京很大, not 北京是大; 是 is reserved for noun-to-noun identity, not general 'to be.'

content.name: teacher
content.side_g: 老师 (lǎoshī) is the respectful term for 'teacher' and is commonly used as a title after a surname, e.g., 王老师 (Wáng lǎoshī, 'Teacher Wang'). It has no plural marker and applies to instructors at all education levels, from kindergarten through university.

content.name: question_particle
content.side_f: Are you a teacher? (lit. 'You are teacher [Q]?')
content.side_g: 吗 (ma) is a sentence-final particle in neutral tone that turns any declarative statement into a yes/no question without changing word order. Unlike 什么 (shénme), which forms content questions asking 'what,' 吗 only asks for confirmation or denial. It also contrasts with 呢 (ne), which raises a contextual follow-up question ('and you?') rather than a polar one.

content.name: not_no
content.side_g: 不 (bù) negates verbs and adjectives and is placed directly before the word it negates (for past actions or 'not have,' use 没 méi instead). Tone sandhi: 不 changes from bù to bú when followed by a 4th-tone syllable, so 不是 is pronounced bú shì, not bù shì. Before 1st, 2nd, or 3rd tones it stays bù.

content.name: student
content.side_e: Wǒ shì xuésheng, nǐ shì lǎoshī ma?
content.side_f: I am a student; are you a teacher?
content.side_g: 学生 (xuésheng) applies to any level of learner — elementary, high school, or university — and pairs naturally with 老师 (teacher) in classroom introductions. Literally 'study-life/person' (学 = to study, 生 = life/person), a pattern seen in many role words. Note the neutral tone on -sheng in the compound (xuésheng, not xuéshēng).

content.name: too_also
content.side_g: 也 is an adverb meaning 'also/too' and must be placed directly before the verb, never after the subject like English 'too' at the end of a sentence. It very commonly pairs with 是 in the pattern 'Subject + 也 + 是 + Noun' to say 'X is also...'. Note that 也 cannot stand alone at the end of a sentence.

content.name: people_person
content.side_f: What nationality are you? (lit. 'You are what person?')
content.side_g: 人 (rén) means 'person' or 'people' and is one of the most productive nouns in Chinese: attach it after a country name to form a nationality, e.g. 中国人 (Chinese person) or 美国人 (American). Chinese nouns do not inflect for number, so 人 serves as both 'person' and 'people' — context or a measure word/number tells you how many.

content.name: china
content.side_f: I am Chinese. (lit. 'I am Middle-Kingdom person.')
content.side_g: 中国 literally means 'Middle Kingdom' (中 zhōng = middle/center, 国 guó = country), reflecting the historical self-conception of China as the central realm. Combine with 人 to form 中国人 (Zhōngguórén), 'Chinese person' — the same 'place + 人' pattern used for any nationality. As a proper noun, Zhōngguó is capitalized in pinyin.

content.name: beijing
content.side_f: I am a Beijinger. (lit. 'Northern Capital person.')
content.side_g: 北京 literally means 'Northern Capital' (北 = north, 京 = capital) and is the capital of 中国. The pattern [city]+人 states where someone is from, so 我是北京人 means 'I'm from Beijing.' Swap in any city to say your own hometown (e.g., 我是上海人).

content.name: america
content.side_f: I am American. (lit. 'I am Beautiful-Country person.')
content.side_g: 美国 (Měiguó) combines 美 'beautiful' + 国 'country' as a phonetic-semantic rendering of 'America' (the 'mei' sound approximates 'A-me-rica'). Add 人 (rén, 'person') to form 美国人 (Měiguó rén), 'American,' exactly parallel to 中国 → 中国人. This country + 人 pattern works for nationalities across the board.

content.name: new_york
content.side_g: Niǔyuē is the phonetic transliteration of 'New York': 纽 (niǔ) approximates 'New' and 约 (yuē) approximates 'York.' The [city]+人 pattern means 'a person from [city],' so 纽约人 = 'New Yorker.' New York is a major city in 美国.
